enrichment.short_description: Haven is a free, open-source, self-hosted blogging platform for sharing private posts, photos and updates with a hand-picked audience of friends and family, with no ads or trackers.
enrichment.seo_title: havenweb Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See what Haven (havenweb.org) offers in 2026: private self-hosted blogging, pricing, key features, pros and cons, and the best alternatives to explore.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Haven, published at havenweb.org, is a self-hostable private blogging platform built as a deliberate alternative to mainstream social media. Rather than posting to a public feed owned by a large company, a Haven user runs their own small website that only the people they explicitly invite can see. The project's tagline captures the pitch directly: your Haven is private, and unlike a public blog or a social network post, search engines and outside companies cannot read what gets published there. The software combines a simple posting interface for writing text, sharing photos and formatting posts in markdown with a built-in private RSS reader, so a Haven owner can both publish to their own small circle and follow other people's Haven sites in one place. Every connection between a visitor's browser and a Haven server is encrypted over HTTPS, and there is intentionally no way to make a Haven public to the entire internet; access is granted account by account to the specific people the owner chooses. Haven was created by an independent developer who wanted a private, low-friction way to share family photos with relatives, and who had grown frustrated with running WordPress for that purpose because of its complexity, plugin maintenance overhead and constant exposure to comment spam and security patching. That frustration led to a from-scratch, deliberately minimal piece of software optimized for exactly one job: quiet, ad-free, algorithm-free sharing with people you already know and trust, rather than growth, engagement or public reach. The project became publicly known after a Show HN post on Hacker News that pitched Haven as a way to run a private website to share with only the people you choose, and it has continued to be maintained and iterated on by its creator and a small community of contributors since. Haven has no venture funding, no acquisition history and no company behind it in the traditional SaaS sense; it is best understood as an independent open-source side project distributed under the MIT license, with source code hosted publicly on GitHub under the havenweb organization. Because there is no corporate parent, there are no employee counts, headquarters or investor rounds to report, and the project's sustainability rests on its creator continuing to maintain the codebase and offer optional paid hosting rather than on outside capital. Technically, Haven runs as a lightweight, self-hosted web application that its creator has specifically designed to run comfortably on modest infrastructure, including a Raspberry Pi sitting on a home network or a small cloud virtual machine on a provider such as Amazon Web Services. Installation is most commonly done through Docker, which packages the application and its dependencies so an owner does not need deep systems administration experience to get a private site running. Because each Haven instance is single-tenant, one server runs exactly one person's or one family's Haven rather than pooling many unrelated users onto shared infrastructure, which is part of how the project preserves both privacy and predictable performance. For people who do not want to manage a server themselves, the project offers an official hosted option: a dedicated virtual server running only that customer's Haven instance, priced at five dollars per month, plus a fully managed hosting tier for people who want the operational burden handled entirely by someone else. Core features center on the writing and reading experience rather than social mechanics. The posting interface supports markdown with a live preview, so users can format text, embed images and structure longer posts without needing to know HTML. Visibility is controlled per account, meaning the owner explicitly creates a login for each friend or family member who should have access, rather than relying on a public link that could be forwarded outside the intended circle. Followers can subscribe to a private RSS feed unique to that Haven, and Haven itself bundles a reader so an owner can follow other people's Havens or traditional RSS feeds inside the same interface they use to publish their own updates. There is no advertising, no algorithmic feed, no public discovery mechanism and no tracking of visitors, which is a conscious design choice rather than an oversight; the entire point of the product is to remove the incentives that push mainstream platforms toward engagement-maximizing design. In terms of audience, Haven is aimed squarely at privacy-conscious individuals and families who want to share photos, life updates or writing with a specific, known group of people, such as extended family scattered across different cities, close friend groups or small private communities, without those updates being visible to search engines, advertisers or the general public. It appeals particularly to technically comfortable users who are willing to run Docker or manage a small VPS in exchange for full control over their data, though the paid hosting tier lowers that bar considerably for less technical users. Competitively, Haven sits in a different niche than mainstream blogging platforms like WordPress.com or Substack, which are built for public reach and, in Substack's case, monetization through paid subscriptions; Haven explicitly rejects that public-by-default model. It also differs from private social sharing done through Facebook groups, Instagram Close Friends or Google Photos shared albums, all of which route the sharing through a large company's servers and business model. Haven's closest philosophical peers are other small, independent, self-hosted publishing tools aimed at digital minimalism and data ownership, and its main tradeoff versus those bigger platforms is exactly the one its creator intended: less reach and fewer built-in social features, in exchange for real privacy, no ads, and full ownership of both the software and the data it stores.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Haven is an open-source, self-hosted blogging platform built for people who want to share photos and updates privately with a specific group of friends or family rather than post publicly. It was created by an independent developer who was frustrated with WordPress's complexity and spam problems and wanted a simpler, private way to share family photos.

Unlike a public blog, a Haven site has no way to be made visible to the general internet. Every visitor needs an account the owner creates for them, every connection is encrypted, and there is no advertising, tracking or algorithmic feed anywhere in the product.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Haven includes a markdown post editor with live preview, per-account access control so the owner decides exactly who can see the site, a private RSS feed for followers, and a built-in RSS reader so owners can follow other Havens or outside blogs from the same interface.

Because each Haven is single-tenant and runs on infrastructure the owner controls or rents, it can run on hardware as small as a Raspberry Pi or a low-cost cloud virtual machine, and it is most commonly deployed using Docker.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Haven's source code is free and open source under the MIT license, so anyone comfortable with Docker can self-host it at no cost beyond their own server. For people who prefer not to manage infrastructure themselves, Haven also offers an official hosted plan on a dedicated virtual server for five dollars per month, plus a fully managed hosting option for those who want setup and maintenance handled for them.
There are no tiered feature plans; the paid options are purely about who manages the hosting, not about unlocking additional functionality.

faqs.question: What is Haven used for?
faqs.answer: Haven is used to run a private, self-hosted blog or photo-sharing site that only people you explicitly invite can see, such as extended family or a close friend group.

faqs.question: Is Haven free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Haven's source code is free and open source under the MIT license. There is also an optional hosted plan starting at 5 dollars a month for people who do not want to manage their own server.

faqs.question: Do I need technical skills to run Haven?
faqs.answer: Self-hosting Haven with Docker is easiest for people comfortable with basic server administration. Less technical users can use Haven's paid or fully managed hosting instead.

faqs.question: Can anyone find my Haven through Google?
faqs.answer: No. Haven has no public visibility mode; only accounts the owner creates can access the site, and it is not designed to be indexed or discoverable.

faqs.question: Who created Haven?
faqs.answer: Haven was created by an independent developer who wanted a simpler, private alternative to WordPress for sharing family photos, and it has been maintained as an open-source project since.

faqs.question: How is Haven different from Substack or WordPress.com?
faqs.answer: Substack and WordPress.com are built for public, discoverable publishing and, in Substack's case, monetization. Haven is built for the opposite use case: small, private, ad-free sharing with people you already know.

enrichment.short_description: Healthchecks.io is an open-source cron job and background task monitoring service that alerts you by email, SMS, Slack or phone call when a scheduled job fails to check in on time.
enrichment.seo_title: healthchecks Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Read our 2026 review of Healthchecks.io: cron and background job monitoring, pricing tiers, self-hosting, pros and cons, and top alternatives compared.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Healthchecks.io is a cron job and background task monitoring service built around the dead man's switch pattern: instead of Healthchecks actively probing your systems, your own scheduled jobs, cron tasks or background workers are configured to send a small HTTP request, a ping, to a unique Healthchecks URL every time they run successfully. If a ping does not arrive within the time window you configured, Healthchecks assumes the job failed silently and immediately alerts you through email, SMS, WhatsApp, phone calls, Slack, PagerDuty, webhooks or dozens of other integrations. This flips the usual monitoring model on its head: rather than monitoring uptime from the outside, it monitors whether your automation actually happened at all, which is exactly the kind of silent failure that traditional uptime monitors miss. The service is the work of a single developer, Pēteris Caune, a long-time Python developer based in Valmiera, Latvia, operating under his one-person company SIA Monkey See Monkey Do. Caune started the project in June 2015 as a side project after concluding that the existing dead man's switch services on the market, chiefly Dead Man's Snitch and later Cronitor, were overpriced for what they offered. He began hacking on a blank Django project that same month, registered the healthchecks.io domain a month later, and publicly launched the service in July 2015. From the outset he made a deliberate strategic choice to open source the entire codebase under a permissive BSD license, both as a differentiator against closed-source competitors and as a trust signal for a product whose entire job is to be reliably notified when something breaks. Nearly a decade later, in a widely read 2024 blog post titled Running One-man SaaS, 9 Years In, Caune described the business as still fundamentally a solo operation, run without outside investors, employees or an office, and profitable as an independent bootstrapped product. There has been no acquisition, funding round or ownership change in the service's history; it remains an independently owned, Latvia-based, one-person SaaS business. Technically, Healthchecks.io is written in Python using the Django web framework, and the full source is available on GitHub, which means that in addition to using the hosted SaaS at healthchecks.io, any organization can clone the repository and run the exact same software on its own infrastructure for free, with no feature gating between the self-hosted and hosted versions of the core monitoring logic. This dual-mode availability, hosted convenience for those who want it and free self-hosting for those who need full data control or have compliance requirements, is one of the product's defining characteristics and a major reason it is popular with developers, sysadmins and DevOps teams who value transparency over black-box monitoring services. Core functionality centers on creating a check for every job you want monitored, each with its own unique ping URL, a configurable expected schedule defined either as a simple time period or as a cron expression, and a grace period that tolerates minor timing variance before triggering an alert. Checks can be grouped, tagged and organized into projects with team access, and each check keeps a log of recent pings so you can audit exactly when a job ran and for how long, since Healthchecks also supports timing pings that report how long a job took to execute, not just whether it ran. Integrations cover the major channels a DevOps team would expect: email is always free and unlimited, while SMS, WhatsApp messages and voice phone calls are metered credits included with paid plans, and webhook, Slack, Microsoft Teams, PagerDuty, Opsgenie and dozens of other integrations are available for routing alerts into existing incident response tooling. On pricing, Healthchecks.io offers a genuinely usable free Hobbyist tier that supports up to 20 monitored checks with 100 log entries retained per check, which is enough for many individual developers or small projects to never need to pay. Above that sits a Supporter tier at 5 dollars a month that keeps the same limits as the free plan but funds the service for users who want to contribute without needing more capacity; a Business tier at 20 dollars a month (or roughly 16 dollars a month billed annually) that raises the limit to 100 checks, 1,000 log entries per check, 50 SMS and WhatsApp credits, 20 phone call credits and email support; and a Business Plus tier at 80 dollars a month (or roughly 64 dollars a month billed annually) that raises the check limit to 1,000, with 500 SMS and WhatsApp credits, 100 phone call credits and priority support. Notably, Caune offers the Business plan free of charge to open-source projects and registered non-profits on request, and the company donates 5 percent of its monthly recurring revenue back to the open-source projects it depends on, largely distributed through GitHub Sponsors. The target audience is squarely developers, DevOps engineers and small-to-midsize engineering teams who run recurring automated jobs, backups, ETL pipelines, cron tasks or scheduled scripts and need to know immediately if one of them silently stops running, rather than discovering the failure days or weeks later. Competitively, Healthchecks.io sits alongside services like Cronitor, Dead Man's Snitch and Better Stack's heartbeat monitoring, but differentiates itself primarily through its fully open-source codebase, self-hosting option, transparent one-person-company operating model, and a free tier generous enough for genuine hobby and small-team use rather than a token trial.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Healthchecks.io monitors cron jobs and other periodic or background tasks using the dead man's switch pattern: your job pings a unique URL every time it runs, and Healthchecks alerts you the moment a ping fails to arrive on schedule. It was created in 2015 by Latvian developer Pēteris Caune, who still runs it as a one-person, bootstrapped SaaS business.

The full codebase is open source under a BSD license and available on GitHub, so teams can either use the hosted service at healthchecks.io or self-host the exact same software on their own infrastructure at no cost.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Each monitored job gets a unique ping URL, a configurable schedule (simple interval or cron expression) and a grace period before an alert fires. Timing pings let you track how long a job takes to run, not just whether it ran.

Alerts route through email (always free), SMS, WhatsApp, phone calls, Slack, PagerDuty, Opsgenie, Microsoft Teams and generic webhooks, making it straightforward to plug Healthchecks into an existing on-call or incident response workflow.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The free Hobbyist plan covers 20 checks with 100 log entries each, enough for many solo developers to never pay. Paid tiers scale up from there: Business at 20 dollars a month (100 checks, SMS/call credits) and Business Plus at 80 dollars a month (1,000 checks, higher credit allowances), both discounted roughly 20 percent on annual billing.
Open-source projects and non-profits can request the Business plan free of charge, and the company donates 5 percent of monthly revenue back to open-source dependencies.

faqs.question: What does Healthchecks.io actually monitor?
faqs.answer: It monitors whether your cron jobs, scheduled scripts and background tasks actually run on time, using a dead man's switch: your job pings a URL on success, and Healthchecks alerts you if that ping doesn't arrive.

faqs.question: Is Healthchecks.io free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Hobbyist plan is free and supports up to 20 checks. Paid plans start at 5 dollars a month for supporters and scale to 20 and 80 dollars a month for higher check limits and SMS/call credits.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Healthchecks.io?
faqs.answer: Yes. The full source code is open source under a BSD license and available on GitHub, so you can run the same software on your own servers instead of using the hosted SaaS.

faqs.question: Who built Healthchecks.io?
faqs.answer: It was built and is still run by Pēteris Caune, a Python developer based in Valmiera, Latvia, as a one-person bootstrapped company that launched the service in July 2015.

faqs.question: Do open-source projects get free access?
faqs.answer: Yes, Healthchecks.io offers the Business plan free of charge to open-source projects and registered non-profits on request.

faqs.question: How is Healthchecks.io different from uptime monitors like Pingdom?
faqs.answer: Uptime monitors check from the outside whether a website responds. Healthchecks.io instead relies on your own jobs checking in, which catches silent failures like a cron job that stops running entirely rather than just a website going down.

enrichment.short_description: Healthie is an API-first, ONC-certified EHR and practice management platform that gives digital health companies scheduling, billing, telehealth and patient engagement tools out of the box.
enrichment.headquarters: New York, New York, United States
enrichment.seo_title: Healthie Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Explore Healthie's API-first EHR platform in 2026: pricing plans, telehealth and billing features, GraphQL API, pros and cons, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Healthie is an API-first electronic health record (EHR) and practice management platform designed specifically for virtual-first and longitudinal care businesses, giving both individual practitioners and digital health startups a fully brandable, HIPAA-compliant infrastructure layer for scheduling, charting, billing, telehealth and patient engagement, accessible either through a ready-made web and mobile interface or entirely through Healthie's own API and SDKs. The company was founded in 2016 by CEO Erica Jain and CTO Cavan Klinsky, and is headquartered in New York City, where it has grown into a company of roughly 150 to 200 employees. Healthie's founding thesis was that a new generation of digital health companies, telehealth startups, coaching platforms, RDs, therapists and hybrid care businesses, needed EHR infrastructure they could embed into their own branded product rather than being forced onto a rigid, legacy, hospital-oriented EHR built for a completely different workflow. That thesis proved durable: by its own reporting, Healthie has grown to serve more than 45,000 clinicians delivering care to over 17 million patients across more than 25 longitudinal care specialties, spanning nutrition, mental health, physical therapy, chronic condition management and other virtual-first care models, and the company has stated it has been profitable for the majority of its history as a company even while raising outside capital. On the funding side, Healthie has raised more than 42 million dollars in total external capital from investors including TCV, Birchmere Ventures, Velvet Sea Ventures and Greymatter, with its most recent disclosed raise being a 23 million dollar Series B led by TCV, announced in October 2024, aimed at continuing to build out its infrastructure for longitudinal, virtual-first healthcare delivery. There has been no acquisition of Healthie by another company; it remains an independent, venture-backed private company. Technically, Healthie is built on a Ruby and PostgreSQL backend with React and React Native powering its web and mobile clients, and native Swift and Java components for iOS and Android respectively, and the platform is ONC Certified, meaning it meets the federal government's certification criteria for health IT used in programs tied to interoperability and quality reporting. Its defining architectural choice is a GraphQL API that exposes essentially the entire platform, scheduling, charting, insurance billing, telehealth, provider management and client engagement, as programmable infrastructure, and this is not a limited public-facing subset: it is described by the company as the same API its own internal teams use to build features across the web and mobile products. To make that API easier to consume, Healthie also ships pre-built React SDKs that give developers ready-made UI components for common workflows like scheduling or messaging, reducing the amount of custom frontend work needed to embed Healthie functionality into a third-party product. Core platform features include appointment scheduling with calendar sync, live video telehealth (including group telehealth sessions on higher tiers), integrated payment processing through Stripe, food and lifestyle journaling tools originally rooted in Healthie's dietitian and nutrition-coaching heritage, customizable intake and assessment forms, care plans and goal tracking, secure messaging between providers and clients, CMS-1500 insurance claim generation, outbound and inbound fax, and a native mobile app available free on both iOS and Android for providers and clients to use on the go. Pricing is structured around four core monthly tiers based primarily on active client count and feature depth. The Core plan starts at 19 dollars a month (18 dollars a month billed annually) and covers up to 10 active clients with scheduling, payments, charting, telehealth and the client portal and mobile app. The Essentials plan, Healthie's most popular tier, costs 49 dollars a month (45 dollars annually) and raises the client cap to 250 while adding free outbound fax, custom forms, CMS-1500 generation and group messaging, with ClaimMD insurance clearinghouse integration available as a 30-dollar-a-month add-on. The Plus plan, at 129 dollars a month (115 dollars annually), removes the client cap entirely and adds group telehealth, online programs and a dedicated inbound and outbound eFax line. The Group plan, starting at 149 dollars a month (135 dollars annually) and scaling with additional clinicians at 50 dollars a month per clinician, is built for multi-provider practices and adds a shared calendar, team member management, roles and permissions, internal staff chat and a dedicated support contact per provider. Across every tier, Healthie includes HIPAA and PCI compliance, core EHR charting, secure messaging, food and lifestyle journaling, care plans and goal tracking, and advanced reporting, with Stripe-processed payments carrying a standard 2.9 percent plus 30 cent transaction fee. The GraphQL API itself, along with more advanced integrations, is offered as an add-on primarily available to customers on the Group and higher-tier or Enterprise-style plans, reflecting Healthie's dual identity as both an off-the-shelf practice management tool for solo and small-group practitioners and a full infrastructure platform, marketed under the Healthie+ name, for digital health startups building their own branded product on top of it. Healthie's target customers span two distinct but related audiences: individual and small-group healthcare practitioners, particularly in nutrition, therapy, coaching and other virtual-first specialties, who want an easy-to-use, affordable, HIPAA-compliant EHR without a long implementation process, and digital health companies, startups and larger care organizations that need to spin up compliant clinical infrastructure quickly and prefer to build on an API rather than negotiate a multi-year contract with a legacy EHR vendor like Epic or athenahealth. Competitively, Healthie is most often compared to other EHR and practice management platforms serving allied health and virtual-first specialties, such as SimplePractice and TherapyNotes on the practitioner-facing side, and to platform infrastructure plays in digital health that offer similar API-first building blocks; Healthie differentiates itself through the breadth of specialties it serves under one roof, its ONC certification, and the fact that the same core platform can be consumed either as a polished ready-made app or as raw, fully programmable infrastructure via GraphQL, letting customers choose how much of the underlying EHR they want to expose to their own end users.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Healthie is an API-first, ONC-certified EHR and practice management platform founded in 2016 by Erica Jain and Cavan Klinsky. It gives individual practitioners a ready-made app for scheduling, charting, billing and telehealth, while also exposing the entire platform through a GraphQL API for digital health companies that want to embed EHR infrastructure into their own branded product.

The company is headquartered in New York and has raised more than 42 million dollars from investors including TCV, Birchmere Ventures and Velvet Sea Ventures, serving more than 45,000 clinicians and 17 million patients across 25-plus longitudinal care specialties.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform covers scheduling, live video telehealth, Stripe-based payment processing, food and lifestyle journaling, customizable intake forms, care plans and goal tracking, secure messaging and CMS-1500 insurance claim generation, all wrapped in a free iOS and Android app for providers and clients.

Its defining feature is a GraphQL API, the same one Healthie's own engineers use internally, paired with pre-built React SDKs, letting digital health startups embed Healthie's EHR functionality directly into their own product rather than sending users to a separate portal.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Healthie prices primarily by active client count across four tiers: Core at 19 dollars a month (up to 10 clients), Essentials at 49 dollars a month (up to 250 clients), Plus at 129 dollars a month (unlimited clients), and Group starting at 149 dollars a month plus 50 dollars per additional clinician for multi-provider practices. Annual billing saves roughly 10 percent.

All plans include HIPAA and PCI compliance, core charting and secure messaging, while the GraphQL API and deeper integrations like ClaimMD or DoseSpot ePrescribing are available as add-ons, typically on Group and higher tiers.

enrichment.short_description: Heap is a digital analytics platform that automatically captures every user interaction on web and mobile products, letting teams analyze behavior retroactively without manually instrumenting events.
enrichment.seo_title: Heap Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Read our 2026 review of Heap by Contentsquare: autocapture analytics features, pricing tiers, session replay, pros and cons, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Heap, now operating as Heap by Contentsquare, is a digital analytics platform best known for pioneering autocapture: instead of requiring engineers to manually write tracking code for every button click, page view or form submission they want to measure, Heap automatically captures every user interaction across a web or mobile product from the moment it is installed, and lets analysts define and retroactively query the specific events they care about after the fact. That reversal of the traditional analytics workflow, capture everything first, define metrics later, was the company's original core insight and remains its primary differentiator against event-based tools that require deliberate, upfront instrumentation. Heap was founded in Silicon Valley in 2013 by Matin Movassate, a former Facebook product manager, and Ravi Parikh, and the company built its headquarters in San Francisco. Over its life as an independent venture-backed company, Heap raised a total of roughly 216 million dollars across six funding rounds, with its final and largest round being a 110 million dollar Series D in December 2021 led by Sixth Street, which valued the company at roughly 960 million dollars just under the widely watched unicorn threshold; other backers over the years included Menlo Ventures, NewView Capital, Goldman Sachs Asset Management and DTCP. That independent chapter ended in late 2023: on September 28, 2023, French customer experience analytics company Contentsquare announced a definitive agreement to acquire Heap, describing it as the largest acquisition in Contentsquare's history, and the deal closed in December 2023. Since then Heap has operated as a product line within Contentsquare rather than as a standalone company, with its go-to-market and roadmap increasingly folded into Contentsquare's broader experience analytics suite; the two companies have publicly framed the combination as bringing together Contentsquare's experience-analytics strength (heatmaps, session replay, frustration signals) with Heap's product-analytics strength (funnels, retention, autocapture-based event analysis) into what they call the future of experience analytics. Technically, Heap's core mechanism relies on a lightweight JavaScript or mobile SDK snippet installed once on a site or app, after which it begins recording every user interaction, clicks, taps, form submissions, page views and more, into a queryable event stream without any per-event code changes. Analysts and product managers can then go into Heap's interface and define virtual events after the fact, essentially telling Heap after the product has already shipped which specific interactions correspond to a meaningful business event, and immediately get historical data for that new definition going back to whenever tracking began, rather than waiting weeks to accumulate new data the way manually instrumented tools require. On top of that raw interaction stream, Heap layers standard product analytics capabilities: funnel analysis to see where users drop off in a multi-step flow, retention analysis to understand which cohorts keep coming back, path analysis to see the sequences users actually take through a product, and user- and account-level analytics for B2B teams that need to see engagement at the account rather than individual level. Session replay and heatmaps are available as an add-on capability layered on top of the core analytics product, letting teams watch recordings of individual sessions or visualize aggregate click and scroll behavior for sessions where replay was enabled, rather than being bundled by default into every tier. Heap's pricing structure has four tiers: Free, Growth, Pro and Premier. The Free tier is genuinely self-serve and requires no sales conversation, covering up to 10,000 monthly sessions with six months of data retention and core autocapture analytics, which is enough for early-stage products or small teams to get real value before ever talking to sales. Every tier above Free, Growth, Pro and Premier, is sold on a custom, contact-sales basis priced primarily on monthly session volume and committed to annually, with reported starting prices for the Growth tier around 3,600 dollars a year for teams that need higher session caps and longer data history. Growth unlocks unlimited users and reports, custom charting, CSV exports and 12 months of history; Pro adds deeper analysis capabilities such as account-level analytics and real-time alerting, with session replay available as a paid add-on; and Premier, the top tier, adds data warehouse integration, behavioral targeting capabilities and a dedicated customer success manager, along with the longest data retention and highest session ceilings. Heap's target customers are product, growth and marketing teams at B2B and B2C software companies who need to understand detailed user behavior, feature adoption, drop-off points and retention drivers without maintaining a large, brittle backlog of manually tagged tracking events, a category of pain particularly acute at companies whose product changes frequently enough that manually instrumented analytics tools constantly fall out of date. Competitively, Heap sits in the product analytics category alongside tools like Mixpanel, Amplitude and PostHog, and its primary differentiation within that set has historically been autocapture-first data collection versus those tools' more event-definition-first approaches, though several competitors have since added their own autocapture options. Since the Contentsquare acquisition, Heap's positioning has shifted further to emphasize its place inside a broader combined suite that pairs product analytics with Contentsquare's session replay, heatmaps and frustration-signal detection, aiming to give customers both the quantitative funnel-and-retention view Heap built its reputation on and the qualitative, visual session-level view that was traditionally Contentsquare's strength, all from one vendor rather than stitching together two separate point solutions.

After raising roughly 216 million dollars and reaching a valuation near 960 million dollars as an independent company, Heap was acquired by Contentsquare in a deal announced in September 2023 and closed in December 2023, and now operates as Heap by Contentsquare within Contentsquare's broader experience analytics suite.

Session replay and heatmaps are available as an add-on for teams that want to pair Heap's quantitative event data with qualitative session recordings, and higher tiers add real-time alerting, data warehouse integration and behavioral targeting.

Higher tiers progressively add unlimited users and reports, longer data retention, account-level analytics, real-time alerts, session replay as an add-on, data warehouse integration and a dedicated customer success manager on the top Premier tier.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Read our 2026 review of Heartbeat.chat: community platform features, pricing plans, branded mobile app, pros and cons, and top alternatives compared.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Heartbeat, marketed at heartbeat.chat, is an all-in-one community platform built for creators, educators and businesses who want to run a paid or free membership community without stitching together a separate chat tool, course platform, events calendar, landing page builder and payment processor. The company was co-founded by Murtaza Bambot, who serves as CEO, and Mayhul Arora, who serves as CTO, and it is headquartered in New York City, where the founders and much of the team are based. Heartbeat's mission, in its own words, is to build tools for an internet defined by belonging, positioning itself against the drift of community and audience engagement toward algorithm-driven, ad-supported social platforms where creators and organizations do not own their audience relationship or their data. The company describes itself as being trusted by roughly 5,000 communities, spanning use cases from creator memberships and coaching programs to internal company or customer communities, and its product philosophy leans heavily on direct founder involvement in customer onboarding, with prospective customers able to join live walkthroughs conducted personally by Heartbeat's founders rather than a generic sales team. Functionally, Heartbeat bundles the core building blocks a community operator would otherwise need to assemble from separate vendors: threaded discussion channels organized by topic, live and recorded events, structured online courses with lesson sequencing, a member directory for discovery and networking within the community, workflow automations that can trigger emails or actions based on member behavior, custom-brandable landing pages for marketing and signups, and native payment processing so a creator can charge for membership access directly inside the platform rather than routing payments through a separate tool like Stripe or a course platform. On top of the web experience, Heartbeat offers a fully branded, white-labeled mobile app on its higher tier, letting a community operator put their own name and icon on an app in the App Store and Google Play rather than sending members to a generic third-party community app. Pricing is structured across three named tiers, each offered with a 14-day free trial that does not require a credit card up front. The Build plan costs 49 dollars a month billed monthly, or 40 dollars a month if billed annually at 490 dollars a year, and covers up to 350 community members, five automation workflows, one custom landing page, unlimited channels, courses and events, a member directory, and basic integrations with tools like Zapier, Zoom and Google Calendar. The Grow plan costs 149 dollars a month billed monthly, or 124 dollars a month if billed annually at 1,490 dollars a year, and raises the member cap to 5,000, removes the automation workflow limit entirely, adds auto-responder and direct-message automation rules, 50 hours of native video hosting, up to 15 custom landing pages, API access for custom integrations, and a one-business-day priority support commitment. The Scale plan costs 849 dollars a month billed monthly, or roughly 766 dollars a month if billed annually at 9,200 dollars a year (a savings of about 989 dollars annually), and is aimed at larger operators: it removes the member cap entirely, adds the fully branded white-label mobile app, advanced analytics, up to 50 custom landing pages, 200 hours of native video hosting, a dedicated onboarding specialist and same-day support. Layered on top of the flat monthly subscription, Heartbeat also takes a transaction fee on payments processed through the platform's built-in payment system, on top of Stripe's own standard processing fees: 5 percent on the Build plan, 2.5 percent on the Grow plan, and 1.25 percent on the Scale plan, meaning the effective cost of running a paid membership community on Heartbeat scales down as a percentage the more a creator invests in a higher tier, which is a deliberate incentive structure to move larger, revenue-generating communities up the pricing ladder. Payment methods supported for members include major credit and debit cards as well as newer options like Cash App Pay and Link, plus various regional bank payment methods, reflecting an emphasis on reducing checkout friction for paying members. Heartbeat's target audience is creators, coaches, course instructors, membership businesses and companies that want to build an owned, monetizable community rather than relying on a free Facebook Group, a Discord server, or a Slack workspace, none of which offer native payments, branded mobile apps or structured course delivery out of the box. Competitively, Heartbeat sits in the same category as platforms like Circle, Mighty Networks, Skool and Discourse, and its differentiation centers on the combination of a fully branded white-label mobile app at the top tier, declining transaction fees as customers scale up plans, and a founder-led onboarding and support experience aimed at helping new community operators launch successfully within a defined timeframe, an approach reflected in Heartbeat's own published guidance on how to launch a paid community within roughly four weeks of signing up.

In addition to the flat subscription, Heartbeat charges a declining transaction fee on payments processed through the platform, 5 percent on Build, 2.5 percent on Grow and 1.25 percent on Scale, on top of Stripe's standard processing fees.

enrichment.short_description: HedgeDoc is a free, open-source, self-hosted markdown editor for real-time collaborative note-taking, with presentation mode, revision history and permission controls.
enrichment.seo_title: HedgeDoc Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See what HedgeDoc offers in 2026: open-source collaborative markdown editing, self-hosting, key features, pros and cons, and the best alternatives compared.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: HedgeDoc is a free, open-source, self-hosted platform for real-time collaborative markdown editing, built for teams, communities and individuals who want a Google-Docs-style collaborative writing experience for markdown notes without handing their content to a third-party company. Multiple people can open the same note simultaneously and see each other's edits appear live, making it a popular choice for meeting notes, technical documentation, workshop hackpads and conference note-taking, especially within open-source, developer and hacker communities that already prefer markdown and self-hosted tooling. The project's history is unusually tangled for open-source software and is worth explaining because it directly shapes what HedgeDoc is today. The lineage traces back to a collaborative markdown editor originally developed under the name HackMD, created primarily by developers Max Wu and Yukai Huang, which existed both as an open-source codebase and, eventually, as a commercial hosted product also called HackMD. To resolve the confusion between the open community codebase and the commercial HackMD product, the open-source project was renamed CodiMD in June 2018 and continued development under the AGPLv3 license as a community-maintained fork, separate from the HackMD company's own commercial roadmap; a hard fork of the CodiMD codebase was created into its own GitHub organization by community maintainers, while the original HackMD team continued maintaining a separate CodiMD-named repository under its own organization for a period. In 2020, the community project renamed itself again, this time to HedgeDoc, specifically to avoid ongoing naming confusion with yet another unrelated project also using the CodiMD name, and HedgeDoc has been the project's identity since. Throughout all of these renamings, the software has remained a community-driven open-source project rather than a venture-backed company; there is no HedgeDoc corporate entity, no funding history, no acquisition and no headquarters to report, and the project is maintained by volunteer contributors coordinating through its GitHub organization. Because of this lineage, HedgeDoc's earliest commits and public availability trace back to around 2016 under the CodiMD name, even though the HedgeDoc name itself only dates to 2020. Technically, HedgeDoc is a self-hosted web application distributed under the AGPL 3.0 license, with its complete source code publicly available on GitHub. It is deliberately lightweight: the project explicitly advertises that it runs smoothly even on a Raspberry Pi, and it is commonly deployed via a pre-configured Docker image or a ready-made server bundle, making it accessible to individuals and small teams without a dedicated infrastructure team. Because there is no hosted commercial SaaS version officially operated by the HedgeDoc project itself in the way HackMD's commercial product is, using HedgeDoc means either self-hosting the open-source software on your own server or finding a third-party host running an instance, which is a meaningfully different distribution model than most commercial note-taking tools. Core features center on real-time collaboration: multiple users can edit the same document simultaneously with changes syncing live across all connected sessions, and every note tracks its revision history so past versions can be reviewed or restored. Beyond plain text editing, HedgeDoc supports rendering of diagrams and graphs directly from markdown syntax, and it includes a presentation mode built on the open-source reveal.js library, letting a written markdown note be instantly turned into a slide deck for live presentations without a separate tool. Access control is handled through a permission system that lets a note's owner decide who can view or edit it, ranging from fully private notes to notes shared with specific collaborators to notes that are freely editable by anyone with the link, giving instances flexibility for both tightly controlled internal documentation and open collaborative hackpad-style use. The entire interface runs in a standard web browser with no client software installation required for end users, only for whoever administers the server. There is no traditional pricing structure to speak of because HedgeDoc has no commercial tiers, subscriptions or paid feature gates; the software itself is entirely free, and the only cost a user incurs is whatever server or hosting infrastructure they choose to run it on, which can range from literally free (a spare Raspberry Pi already on a home network) to whatever a cloud VM costs per month. HedgeDoc's target audience is technically comfortable teams, open-source communities, developer groups, universities, hacker and maker spaces, and privacy- or self-hosting-conscious individuals who want collaborative markdown editing without routing their notes through a third-party commercial service, plus organizations with data residency or compliance requirements that make self-hosting attractive or mandatory. Competitively, HedgeDoc sits closest to the commercial HackMD product it originally diverged from, as well as to other real-time collaborative editors like Etherpad, and to broader note-taking and documentation tools like Notion or Google Docs, though those alternatives are markdown-optional, hosted-only, closed-source commercial products rather than markdown-first, self-hostable, AGPL-licensed open-source software. HedgeDoc's core differentiation is precisely that combination: it is free, it is fully open source, it can be run entirely on infrastructure the user controls, and it is purpose-built around markdown and developer-friendly workflows like slide generation from notes, rather than being a general-purpose document editor that happens to support markdown as one input format among many.

paragraphs: HedgeDoc is a free, open-source, self-hosted platform for real-time collaborative markdown editing. Its lineage traces back to the HackMD open-source codebase around 2016, which was renamed CodiMD in 2018 to separate the community project from the commercial HackMD product, and then renamed again to HedgeDoc in 2020.

There is no company behind HedgeDoc; it is a volunteer-maintained, community-driven open-source project distributed under the AGPL 3.0 license, with no hosted commercial SaaS version operated by the project itself.

A flexible permission system controls who can view or edit each note, from fully private to freely link-editable, and the whole platform is deliberately lightweight, running comfortably even on a Raspberry Pi via Docker.

faqs.question: Can I self-host HedgeDoc?
faqs.answer: Yes, HedgeDoc is designed to be self-hosted, commonly via Docker, and is lightweight enough to run on hardware as small as a Raspberry Pi.

faqs.question: Does HedgeDoc support real-time collaboration?
faqs.answer: Yes, multiple users can edit the same markdown note at the same time, with changes syncing live and full revision history available.

faqs.question: Can HedgeDoc create presentations?
faqs.answer: Yes, HedgeDoc includes a built-in presentation mode powered by reveal.js that turns a markdown note directly into a slide deck.

faqs.question: Who maintains HedgeDoc?
faqs.answer: HedgeDoc is maintained by a community of volunteer open-source contributors rather than a commercial company, coordinated through its GitHub organization.

use_cases.title: Collaborative meeting and workshop notes
use_cases.description: Teams and conference attendees use HedgeDoc as a shared hackpad to take collaborative notes in real time during meetings or workshops.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted technical documentation
use_cases.description: Developer teams self-host HedgeDoc to write and collaboratively edit markdown documentation without relying on a third-party SaaS tool.

use_cases.title: Markdown-based slide presentations
use_cases.description: A presenter writes talk notes in markdown and uses HedgeDoc's built-in presentation mode to deliver a live slideshow directly from the note.

features: Runs via Docker or a server bundle

enrichment.short_description: Height was a fast, keyboard-driven, AI-assisted project management tool for engineering teams; the service was discontinued and shut down permanently on September 24, 2025.
enrichment.headquarters: New York, New York, United States
enrichment.seo_title: Height Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives (Discontinued)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Height.app shut down in September 2025. Read our review of its features, pricing history, founders and the best alternatives to consider instead.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Height was a project management and issue-tracking tool built for software teams, notable for a fast, keyboard-driven interface and, in its later years, for embedding AI directly into project management workflows rather than bolting AI on as an afterthought. The company was founded in 2018 by Michael Villar, who served as CEO, and was headquartered in New York City. Before starting Height, Villar had already been through a notable prior exit: he co-founded Kickoff, a productivity company that was acquired by Stripe in 2013, and he subsequently worked as an early product designer and full-stack engineer at Stripe, where he helped build widely used products including Stripe Checkout, Stripe's iPhone app and Stripe Atlas. That background shaped Height's product philosophy toward tight, fast, engineering-team-friendly tooling rather than a broad, generalist project management suite. Height raised institutional funding, including a 14 million dollar Series A led by Redpoint Ventures announced in 2021, bringing the company's total disclosed funding to roughly 18.3 million dollars over its lifetime; it was not, based on available public information, a Y Combinator company. As a product, Height distinguished itself from heavier competitors like Jira and Asana through speed and a UI built around command-palette navigation, bulk editing and pervasive keyboard shortcuts, an approach the company positioned as noticeably snappier than the sluggish feel some users associated with larger, more established tools. Height's most distinctive later-stage feature was its AI assistant, which had full context on a team's project data and could summarize task history, draft task descriptions and surface likely blockers directly inside the workflow rather than as a generic chatbot layered on top. For engineering teams specifically, Height offered AI-driven bug triage, in which incoming bug reports could be automatically assessed for likely priority based on keywords and context and then routed to the appropriate engineer or team, reducing the manual triage overhead that many engineering teams handle by hand. The product's free tier was notably generous for its category, supporting up to five users with unlimited tasks and lists and full access to AI features at no cost, which was one of the more permissive free plans among project management tools at the time. Paid pricing sat at 8.50 dollars per user per month for the Teams tier and 14 dollars per user per month for the Business tier, with Height integrating with common tools in the engineering and design toolchain, including Figma, as part of its workflow. In March 2025, after seven years of operation, Height announced that it would shut down, with CEO Michael Villar describing the decision as one of the hardest he had to make; the company gave existing customers a runway before the shutdown and the service's final day of operation was September 24, 2025. Public reporting at the time framed the closure around Height doubling down on a new direction in autonomous project management before ultimately deciding to wind the product down entirely rather than pivot the existing product; there is no publicly reported acquisition of Height by another company, and no indication that the Height product, brand or codebase continued operating under new ownership after the shutdown date. As of today, Height.app is not available as an active product, and prospective customers researching Height should treat any pricing, feature or trial information as historical rather than current, since new signups and continued service are no longer possible through the company's own website or infrastructure. For historical and comparative purposes, Height's target audience was software engineering teams and fast-moving startups that wanted a lighter-weight, faster, more keyboard-centric alternative to Jira, Linear or Asana, with AI-assisted triage and task management layered directly into the core workflow rather than treated as a bolt-on feature. Competitively during its lifetime, Height was most frequently compared to Linear, Jira, Asana, ClickUp and Notion's project management features, and it differentiated itself primarily on raw interface speed, keyboard-first interaction design, and how deeply its AI assistant was integrated into everyday task and bug management rather than positioned as a separate add-on module. Teams that previously relied on Height for project management have needed to migrate to an alternative tool since the September 2025 shutdown, and Height's closure is frequently cited in industry commentary as an example of how even well-funded, well-reviewed developer tools can fail to reach sustainable scale in a crowded project management market dominated by entrenched incumbents like Jira, Asana and the fast-growing Linear.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Height was a fast, keyboard-driven project management and issue-tracking tool founded in 2018 by Michael Villar, a former Stripe product designer and engineer, and headquartered in New York City. The company raised roughly 18.3 million dollars in total funding, including a 14 million dollar Series A led by Redpoint Ventures.

Height is no longer operating. The company announced its shutdown in March 2025 and ceased service permanently on September 24, 2025, after roughly seven years in business. There is no public record of an acquisition; the product was discontinued outright.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Height was built around speed: command-palette navigation, bulk editing and keyboard shortcuts throughout the interface, positioned as noticeably faster than tools like Jira and Asana. Its most distinctive feature was a built-in AI assistant with full project context that could summarize task history, draft descriptions and surface blockers.
For engineering teams, Height also offered AI-driven bug triage, automatically assessing incoming bug reports for priority and routing them to the right engineer or team.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Before its shutdown, Height offered a generous free plan for up to 5 users with unlimited tasks, lists and full AI features included. Paid tiers were priced at 8.50 dollars per user per month for Teams and 14 dollars per user per month for Business.
This pricing is historical only. Height is no longer accepting new signups or providing service; anyone researching it today should look at active alternatives such as Linear, Jira or Asana instead.

faqs.question: Is Height.app still available?
faqs.answer: No. Height announced it was shutting down in March 2025 and the service permanently stopped operating on September 24, 2025. It is no longer available for new or existing customers.

faqs.question: Who founded Height?
faqs.answer: Height was founded in 2018 by Michael Villar, who previously co-founded Kickoff (acquired by Stripe in 2013) and worked as a product designer and engineer at Stripe.

faqs.question: Was Height acquired?
faqs.answer: There is no public record of Height being acquired by another company. It was discontinued outright by its own team rather than absorbed into another product.

faqs.question: What should I use instead of Height now?
faqs.answer: Since Height has shut down, former users have migrated to alternatives such as Linear, Jira, Asana or ClickUp, depending on team size and workflow needs.

enrichment.short_description: Helicone is an open-source LLM observability platform and AI gateway that logs, monitors and helps optimize the cost and performance of applications built on OpenAI, Anthropic and other LLM providers.
enrichment.seo_title: Helicone Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Read our 2026 review of Helicone: open-source LLM observability and AI gateway features, pricing tiers, the Mintlify acquisition, pros, cons and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Helicone is an open-source LLM observability platform and AI gateway built to give developers a unified, one-line-of-code way to monitor, log, evaluate and optimize the cost and performance of applications built on large language models. Rather than requiring teams to build custom logging and cost-tracking pipelines around every LLM provider they use, Helicone sits as a proxy or SDK integration between an application and providers like OpenAI, Anthropic and others, capturing every request and response so teams get a unified view of latency, cost, token usage, error rates and user-level interaction data across all the LLM traffic flowing through their product. The company was founded in 2023 by Justin Torre and Cole Gottdank, and it went through Y Combinator's Winter 2023 batch, a credential that gave it early visibility and access to a network of AI-native startups that became some of its earliest and most demanding customers as LLM applications scaled in production. Helicone built its reputation on being genuinely open source, with its full platform code publicly available on GitHub, which mattered to a developer audience that was wary of routing sensitive prompt and completion data through a fully closed-source, black-box SaaS layer sitting between them and their model provider. Over roughly three years as an independent company, Helicone grew to process what the company describes as 14.2 trillion tokens and to serve over 16,000 organizations, spanning solo developers experimenting with LLM APIs up to funded AI-native startups running significant production traffic. In March 2026, Helicone's independent chapter ended: Mintlify, the AI-era documentation and developer-knowledge-infrastructure company, announced it had acquired Helicone, with Helicone's founders Justin Torre and Cole Gottdank joining Mintlify's San Francisco team. The stated rationale on both sides centered on Helicone already powering the AI interactions inside Mintlify's own products before the deal, and the acquisition being framed by both companies as combining Helicone's observability, cost tracking and routing infrastructure with Mintlify's broader push into AI-era knowledge infrastructure, unlocking more accurate and performant AI services, deeper analytics across AI interactions, and integrated multi-provider routing and fallback handling for Mintlify's platform. Following the acquisition, Helicone has continued operating in what the company describes as maintenance mode, meaning it keeps shipping security updates, bug fixes and support for new models, but its independent product roadmap and go-to-market motion are now folded into Mintlify's broader strategy rather than continuing as a fully separate standalone business. Technically, Helicone's core product functions as an AI gateway and observability layer: developers can integrate it either as a lightweight proxy that requests are routed through, or via SDKs and direct API integrations, and Helicone then captures structured logs of every request, including prompts, completions, latency, token counts, and derived cost figures calculated per model and provider. On top of raw logging, Helicone offers evaluation tooling to test and score model outputs, a query language called HQL (Helicone Query Language) for more advanced analysis of logged data on paid tiers, alerting for anomalies or cost spikes, and multi-provider routing and fallback handling so an application can automatically shift traffic between providers or models if one becomes unavailable or degraded. Pricing follows a four-tier structure. The Hobby tier is free and is designed for developers starting new AI projects, including 10,000 requests per month, 1 gigabyte of log storage, a single user seat and 7-day data retention, enough for early prototyping and small side projects without any cost. The Pro tier costs 79 dollars a month and targets growing development teams, offering unlimited seats, alerting, reporting and access to the HQL query language, with a base allotment of requests included and additional usage billed beyond that; it also extends data retention to one month and raises the log ingestion rate to roughly 1,000 events per minute. The Team tier costs 799 dollars a month and is aimed at scaling organizations, supporting up to five organizations under one account, SOC-2 and HIPAA compliance, three months of data retention and a log ingestion rate around 15,000 events per minute. The top Enterprise tier is custom-priced and adds a tailored master service agreement, SAML single sign-on, on-premises deployment options, forever data retention, the highest log ingestion ceilings around 30,000 events per minute, and volume-based discounting. Helicone also runs several discount programs layered on top of the standard tiers: startups under two years old with less than 5 million dollars in funding get 50 percent off their first year, non-profits receive discounts scaled to organization size, open-source projects can receive a 100-dollar annual credit, and students get complimentary access. Helicone's target audience is developers and engineering teams building production applications on top of LLM APIs, spanning solo indie developers who want free visibility into their OpenAI or Anthropic usage up through funded AI-native startups and larger organizations that need SOC-2 and HIPAA-compliant observability, cost governance and multi-provider routing at meaningful scale. Competitively, Helicone sits in the LLM observability and AI gateway category alongside tools like LangSmith, Langfuse, Portkey and Datadog's LLM observability features, and its core differentiation has been its open-source codebase, one-line-of-code integration model, and its combined identity as both an observability platform and an AI gateway capable of routing and fallback, rather than being purely a passive logging and analytics layer bolted on after the fact.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Helicone is an open-source LLM observability platform and AI gateway founded in 2023 by Justin Torre and Cole Gottdank, and a graduate of Y Combinator's Winter 2023 batch. It gives developers a unified way to log, monitor, evaluate and optimize the cost and performance of applications built on OpenAI, Anthropic and other LLM providers, with source code publicly available on GitHub.

In March 2026, Helicone was acquired by Mintlify, the AI-era documentation and knowledge-infrastructure company, after growing to process 14.2 trillion tokens for more than 16,000 organizations as an independent company. Helicone now operates in maintenance mode, continuing security updates and new model support under Mintlify.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Helicone functions as both a passive observability layer and an active AI gateway: it logs every prompt, completion, latency figure and calculated cost across LLM providers, and it can route and fall back traffic across multiple providers or models. Advanced users get access to HQL, Helicone's dedicated query language for analyzing logged AI traffic.
The platform also includes evaluation tooling to test and score model outputs, alerting for cost spikes or anomalies, and compliance features like SOC-2 and HIPAA support on higher tiers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Helicone's free Hobby tier includes 10,000 requests a month, 1 GB of storage and a single seat, with 7-day data retention. Paid tiers scale from Pro at 79 dollars a month (unlimited seats, HQL, 1-month retention) to Team at 799 dollars a month (SOC-2 and HIPAA, 3-month retention) up to custom-priced Enterprise with SAML SSO, on-premises deployment and forever retention.
Discounts are available for early-stage startups (50 percent off the first year), non-profits, open-source projects (100-dollar annual credit) and students.

faqs.question: What does Helicone do?
faqs.answer: Helicone is an LLM observability platform and AI gateway that logs, monitors and helps optimize the cost and performance of applications built on LLM providers like OpenAI and Anthropic, with a single line of code.

faqs.question: Is Helicone free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Helicone's Hobby tier is free and includes 10,000 requests a month, 1 GB of log storage and 7-day data retention. Paid plans start at 79 dollars a month for the Pro tier.

faqs.question: Is Helicone open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, Helicone's full platform code is open source and publicly available on GitHub, which also allows for self-hosting.

faqs.question: Who founded Helicone?
faqs.answer: Helicone was founded in 2023 by Justin Torre and Cole Gottdank, and it went through Y Combinator's Winter 2023 (W23) batch.

faqs.question: Was Helicone acquired?
faqs.answer: Yes, Helicone was acquired by Mintlify, the AI-era documentation and knowledge infrastructure company, in a deal announced in March 2026. Helicone now operates in maintenance mode under Mintlify.

faqs.question: Does Helicone support routing between multiple LLM providers?
faqs.answer: Yes, Helicone functions as an AI gateway that can route traffic and handle fallback across multiple LLM providers and models, in addition to its observability and logging features.
